title: God's Vindication, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-12-24 -AM- God's Vindication - Part 2 - 1 Peter 3_18_18-22 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Three Examples of God's Faithful Vindication
subpoints: Review of last week's four interpretive principles: purpose of 1 Peter, Scripture does not contradict itself, semantic range of "salvation," context is king
The main point: God vindicates everyone who suffers unjustly for the sake of the gospel
Peter gives three examples to prove this: Christ's vindication in the resurrection, Noah's vindication through the flood, our vindication in baptism
Two distracting statements: "baptism now saves" and "Christ preached to spirits in prison" — must not derail us from the main point

outline.title: First Example: God Vindicated Christ in the Resurrection
subpoints: Verse 18: Christ suffered unjustly — the just for the unjust — put to death in the flesh
Christ Himself entrusted to the Father who always judges righteously (1 Peter 2:22-23)
God vindicated Jesus by raising Him from the dead by the power of the Holy Spirit
The resurrection affirmed that Christ's suffering was righteous and that He indeed brought us to God
The cross seems a place of defeat but through it all powers were subjected to Christ (Colossians 2:15, Hebrews 2:14-15)

outline.title: Second Example: God Vindicated Noah Through the Waters of the Flood
subpoints: Peter calls Noah a preacher of righteousness — Noah suffered 120 years at the hands of history's most wicked generation
Genesis 6: the wickedness of mankind was great; God gave 120 years for repentance while Noah built the ark
The "spirits in prison" are the souls of those who were alive in Noah's time but refused to repent — now in hell
Christ preached through the Holy Spirit through Noah to that generation during those 120 years
Hebrews 11:7: Noah condemned the world by building the ark — the construction itself was a proclamation
Noah faithfully entrusted God and did good; the flood came and vindicated him — God's "I told you so"
Application: if God sustained Noah through 120 years of suffering the most wicked generation, He can sustain you

outline.title: Third Example: God Vindicates Us Through Our Baptism
subpoints: There is no such thing as an unbaptized Christian in the Bible — baptism is the public declaration of belonging to Christ
In the early church and in persecuted contexts today, baptism marks you as belonging to Christ and brings suffering
Verse 21: baptism is an "appeal" to God for a good conscience — not a mere ritual washing
The correspondence (antitype): as Noah was vindicated through water, the Christian is vindicated through water
Baptism saves in the sense that it is an appeal to God that this person belongs to Christ and is suffering unjustly
Verse 22: Christ is at the right hand of God — He sees our "wet head" and a clear conscience, and will vindicate us if not in this life then in the life to come

summary:
In this second sermon on 1 Peter 3:18-22, Pastor Tito Lyro unfolds the three examples that Peter provides to prove that God vindicates those who suffer unjustly for righteousness' sake. After reviewing last week's four interpretive principles, he focuses on the main point that the two controversial statements — "baptism now saves" and "Christ preached to spirits in prison" — are not the point of the passage. They are examples within a broader argument: because God has always vindicated the faithful who suffer unjustly, Christians today can entrust themselves to God and continue doing good.

The first example is Christ's own vindication in the resurrection. Christ suffered unjustly — the just for the unjust — entrusting Himself to the Father who judges righteously. God vindicated Him by raising Him from the dead by the power of the Holy Spirit, affirming that all Christ accomplished on the cross was accepted and effective. The cross seemed a place of defeat, yet through it all principalities and powers were subjected to Christ.

The second example is Noah's vindication through the waters of the flood. Peter calls Noah a preacher of righteousness who endured 120 years of suffering at the hands of history's most wicked generation. During that time, Christ preached through the Holy Spirit through Noah to the people, giving them ample opportunity to repent — which they refused. Those spirits are now in prison (hell), not because God was mean but because they rejected the gracious offer of repentance. Noah faithfully trusted God and built the ark; the flood came and vindicated him completely. If God sustained Noah through that extreme situation, He can sustain us in our comparatively lesser suffering.

The third example is the Christian's own vindication through baptism. Baptism in the New Testament was the public declaration that a person now belonged to Christ, which immediately brought suffering. It is not a ritual washing but an appeal to God — a pledge that this person belongs to Christ and suffers unjustly. Just as Noah was vindicated through water, the Christian is vindicated through the waters of baptism. Christ, now seated at the right hand of God with all authorities and powers subject to Him, sees the baptized believer and will vindicate them if not in this life then in the life to come.
transcript:
## Introduction: Three Examples of God's Faithful Vindication

For the reading of God's Word, we're turning to 1 Peter chapter 3, reading verses 18 through 22.

"For Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh but made alive by the Spirit, by whom also He went and preached to the spirits in prison, who formerly were disobedient when once the divine longsuffering waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was being prepared, in which a few, that is, eight souls, were saved through water. There is also an antitype which now saves us — baptism — not the removal of the filth of the flesh, but the answer of a good conscience toward God through the resurrection of Jesus Christ, who has gone into heaven and is at the right hand of God, and angels and authorities and powers have been made subject to Him."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Our glorious God, we thank you that You speak, and You speak through Your Word. Open our hearts, our eyes, and our ears to hear wonderful things concerning You this morning. In Jesus's name, amen. Please be seated.

We have been working through 1 Peter for a while now. We took a hiatus over Christmas season with a few Advent series, then some more time to work on this particular passage. After all that, it resulted in four sermons. So what initially was meant to be one sermon now morphed into four sermons. We started on it last Lord's Day, starting with verse eight, so if you were here last Lord's Day I would encourage you to go back and listen to the message from last week.

What we're going to do today is take a look at verses 18 through 22 and understand how they fit in the rest of 1 Peter, what we should believe concerning Christ from these verses, and how we should live because of them.

As we read these verses, there are two statements that Peter makes that may distract us from understanding how these verses fit in the rest of the passage and from the main point that Peter is making in this whole passage starting in verse 8 going all the way into chapter 4 verse 6. The main point is that God vindicates everyone who suffers unjustly. That's the point that Peter is making in this greater passage — God vindicates everyone who suffers unjustly for the sake of the gospel. To put it in simpler terms, as we saw last week: God has our back.

The two distracting statements are Peter's claim that "baptism now saves" — which shakes our Protestant sensibilities — and his statement that Jesus preached to spirits in prison, which seems out of our theological categories.

So let us remind ourselves of the four principles we saw last week. First, every passage should say something about the theme of the book. The purpose of 1 Peter is to encourage Christians who are suffering for the sake of Christ to continue following Him and doing good, in order not to grow bitter towards God and towards those causing them to suffer. Second, the Scriptures do not contradict themselves, so we interpret every passage in light of all passages. Therefore, in the case of baptism, we must affirm that baptism indeed saves, but not in the sense of redeeming a person from spiritual death to spiritual life — because the whole Bible tells us that redemption is by faith alone, grace alone, in Christ alone. Third, the words "save" and "salvation" have a semantic range — they can describe everything from new birth to glorification. And fourth, context is king — the role of verses 18 through 22 is to provide three examples to back up what Peter has been teaching: that God vindicates those who suffer unjustly for righteousness' sake.

## First Example: God Vindicated Christ in the Resurrection

What is Peter doing here? He is saying: do you want proof that God is going to vindicate you? Look at Christ. Christ was vindicated in the resurrection.

Verse 18: "For Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh but made alive by the Spirit."

Christ suffered unjustly for doing good. That's what happened at the crucifixion — the suffering of the just, the righteous, for the unjust, the unrighteous. Peter already said in chapter 2, verses 22 and 23, that Christ was reviled against and did not revile back, because Christ entrusted Himself to the Father who always judges righteously — which is really the theme of 1 Peter. Christ Himself saw reason to entrust Himself to God, to suffer unjustly, because He knew that the Father would vindicate Him.

And that's exactly what happened. God vindicated Jesus. What does vindication mean? To clear someone of blame or suspicion, to deliver, to rescue, to bring vengeance. So God vindicated Jesus by raising Him from the dead by the power of the Holy Spirit, and by doing that God affirmed that Christ indeed brought us to God, that His suffering was righteous and effective. Christ suffered on the cross at the hands of the wicked; God said, "Jesus was right in His obedience," and brought Him back to life, vindicating all that He had suffered.

The cross seems to be a place of defeat, a place where Christ lost the battle. Yet it is the very thing that subjected all power to Him. As Paul says in Colossians 2:15, speaking of the crucifixion: "Having disarmed principalities and powers, He made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in it." And Hebrews 2:14-15: "That through death He might destroy him who had the power of death, that is, the devil, and release those who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage."

The cross is always validated by the resurrection. If Christ died and stayed dead, all that He would be is a guy who died on a cross. But the fact that He rose from the dead is what validates that the cross worked. Paul says that if Christ did not rise, we would be of all people most to be pitied. But our Savior is not dead — He is alive. He came back to life and because of that, the cross is validated. Paul refers to that in Ephesians 1:19-23: "What is the exceeding greatness of His power toward us who believe, according to the working of His mighty power which He worked in Christ when He raised Him from the dead and seated Him at His right hand in the heavenly places, far above all principality and power and might and dominion, and every name that is named... and He put all things under His feet and gave Him to be head over all things to the church."

## Second Example: God Vindicated Noah Through the Waters of the Flood

Now look at verses 19 and 20. "By whom also He went and preached to the spirits in prison, who formerly were disobedient when once the divine longsuffering waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was being prepared, in which a few, that is, eight souls, were saved through water."

Noah has a special place in Peter's theology. Peter refers to him again in 2 Peter 2:5 and calls Noah a preacher of righteousness. There Peter says: "God did not spare the ancient world, but saved Noah, one of eight people, a preacher of righteousness, bringing in the flood on the world of the ungodly."

Now in order for the Noah reference to make sense to us as an example of God's vindication of the righteous, we have to make sense of the spirits in prison in verse 19. The spirits are the souls of people who were alive in the time of Noah but refused to repent. And the prison is hell — they are paying for their refusal to repent and believe in God.

But if you read from the New King James, you notice that in verse 18, the word "Spirit" has a capital S. The New King James capitalizes it to indicate that Jesus was raised from the dead by the power of the Holy Spirit. And it is by that same Spirit in verse 19 that He preached to these spirits in prison.

Now there are three questions that arise. One: when did this preaching take place? Two: who are the spirits? And three: what is this prison?

I think the best interpretation is that this preaching took place at the time of Noah, when he was building the ark. In the Holy Spirit, through Noah, Christ preached at the time of Noah to the people around him. Now, the spirits here are the souls of those who were alive while Noah was building the ark.

The Bible speaks of Noah's generation as the most wicked generation to ever live on earth. Turn to Genesis chapter 6, verses 1 through 3, then drop down to verse 5. Here we have the sons of God — the Godly line of Seth that had been promised that through that line the Messiah would come — becoming enamored with the daughters of men, the wicked line of Cain. That unholy union eventually brought the destruction of the whole world.

Dropping down to verse 5: "Then the Lord saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually." And in verse 6: "And the Lord was sorry that He had made man on the earth, and He was grieved in His heart."

But notice in verse 3: "My Spirit shall not strive with man forever... his days shall be one hundred and twenty years." This doesn't mean man would only live 120 years — people continued to live 900 years after this. The 120 years is the time between now and when God would destroy the world. God was giving even that most wicked generation 120 years to repent while Noah built the ark.

Now Peter calls Noah a preacher of righteousness, and the author of Hebrews says that Noah condemned the world by building the ark. Hebrews 11:7: "By faith Noah, being divinely warned of things not yet seen, moved with godly fear, prepared an ark for the saving of his household, by which he condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness which is according to faith." Noah's building the ark itself — that construction — was already a proclamation to the world. Their lack of belief in what Noah was saying and doing ended up with their devastation.

This preaching taking place while Noah builds the ark becomes even clearer when we consider two things from verse 20. One: these spirits were "formerly" disobedient — not now, but in a former time. Two: the divine longsuffering was displayed in the days of Noah, during those 120 years, in which time that generation had plenty of opportunity to repent, and yet they chose not to, and were wiped out by the judgment of God.

So: when did this preaching take place? At the days of Noah — Christ by His Holy Spirit spoke through Noah to that generation. Who are the spirits? The souls of people who were alive in the time of Noah but refused to repent. And what is the prison? Hell — they are there paying for their refusal to repent.

Brothers and sisters, do you realize that the only thing that gets you to hell is failing to repent and believe in the Lord Jesus Christ? Every moment that you have not believed in Him is a sign of God's infinite grace, giving you the opportunity to repent and turn to Him for salvation. The generation of Noah did not do that. And now they are in hell — not because God is mean, but because they were wicked and refused to take the free gift He had given them.

So how is this an encouragement to us? For 120 years, Noah suffered at the hands of the most wicked generation known to men for doing what God had called him to do. During that same time, Noah continued to do good — building the ark — and trusted that God would take care of the rest. Yes, there was no water, there was no rain, there was no precedent — but if God said it was going to happen, Noah trusted it would happen. And for 120 years he faithfully entrusted to God that He was going to take care of His own promises, and did good by building the ark.

One day, the waters of the flood came and through these waters Noah was saved — that is, God vindicated him from all he had suffered for righteousness' sake. This was the divine "I told you so" on Noah's behalf. Those waters came and Noah was right. Noah's faith became sight and he was vindicated. The wicked was punished. The righteous was vindicated.

And if God gave Noah the grace to trust Him while suffering at the hands of the most wicked generation known to man — can He not do the same for you? Can He not give you grace to suffer in the hands of the wicked? Can He not strengthen you to do good even though you're being punished for doing good? Can He not sustain you as you trust your life to Him? Of course He can — because this is an argument from the greater to the lesser. Noah's situation was a much more difficult situation than yours. If God sustained Noah then, He can sustain you now in a lesser situation. His grace is sufficient for us.

## Third Example: God Vindicates Us Through Our Baptism

Now the third and most personal one — we are vindicated in our baptism. Look at verse 21: "There is also an antitype which now saves us — baptism — not the removal of the filth of the flesh, but the answer of a good conscience toward God through the resurrection of Jesus Christ."

God vindicates His people through their baptism. The first thing to notice is that Peter assumed that those who were part of these churches were baptized. There is no such thing as an unbaptized Christian in the Bible. You either are Christian or you are unbaptized — you cannot be both. In the first-century church, it was baptism — not walking an aisle or signing a card or saying a prayer — that publicly declared that a person was a Christian, part of the church of the Lord Jesus Christ. And that automatically brought suffering because the world was against Christ.

This is still the case in places where Christianity is not an accepted religion, especially in Muslim contexts. A person may talk about Jesus, may even dare to say they believe what the Bible says about Him, but when they get baptized, there is no going back. If you're a Muslim and you get baptized with Christian baptism, you cannot go back to Islam. Sadly, Muslims understand Christian baptism better than most evangelicals do.

And Peter says there is a connection between Noah's being saved through the waters of the flood and our baptism — that's what the word "antitype" means. There is a correspondence, a fulfillment. The analogy is this: as Noah was vindicated through water, so the Christian is vindicated through water.

But Peter makes clear that this baptism is not simply a ritual washing — not just the removal of dirt from the body. He describes it as an appeal to God, an action in relation to God. The ESV reads: "Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you, not as a removal of dirt from the body, but as an appeal to God for a good conscience through the resurrection of Jesus Christ."

So to tie it all together: how does baptism save? The answer has to be that it saves in the same way that Noah was saved through the water of the flood, because that's what this corresponds to. Noah was saved — vindicated — from the suffering he experienced at the hands of the world as he lived in obedience to God. In what way does baptism save the Christian? The Christian is vindicated by the waters of baptism. The waters of baptism are an appeal to God — that the one baptized belongs to God and has a clear conscience that he is suffering unjustly.

Brothers and sisters, are you suffering unjustly because of your faith in Christ? Are you wondering if God is going to do anything about it? You have been sealed with the sign of the new covenant, of the covenant of grace. You have been ingrafted into Christ. God is doing something about it through the resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ. Our Lord, to whom we are united by baptism, is in heaven, ruling over all things. Look at verse 22 — He sees you, He sees your wet head and clear conscience, and He vindicates you if not in this life then in the life to come.

Brothers and sisters, we have been baptized and as it were we are in the ark right now. We are going through the waters. And the ark was not a pleasant experience — 13 months in a box with every animal, with one window on top. Being thrown about all over the place. That's where we are right now. Life is shaking us about all around, like the water did to Noah. But at the end, the waters will take us to a resting place. Remember what happened to the waters of the flood? They brought Noah to Mount Ararat and he found rest there and he was vindicated and life continued. That is true of us as well.

Christ was vindicated in the resurrection. Noah was vindicated through the waters of the flood. You and I are vindicated in our union with the risen Savior, signified by our baptism.

We're going to sing "Be Still My Soul" again today — the same hymn as last week — because we need to be reminded again and again that the Lord is on our side, that through thorny ways He leads us to a joyful end, that the waves and winds that knock us about still know His voice, that Jesus can repay all that in this life He takes away, that the hour is indeed hastening on when we shall forever be with the Lord. Brothers and sisters, entrust your soul to the Lord Jesus Christ and do good, for He will vindicate you if not in this life then in the life to come.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you that You are a God who speaks, and You speak through Your word. We pray that the word proclaimed today will be a sound word, and that whatever was true would find fertile grounds in our hearts, that it might grow and produce through the hundredfold. We ask in Jesus's name, amen.
